title: Green Eye Discharge in Cats: Causes, Urgency, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Noticed green pus in your cat's eye? Learn about bacterial infections, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos help your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Green Eye Discharge?

Green or yellow discharge, often referred to as pus, is a sign that your cat’s immune system is responding to an infection. Unlike clear tearing, which can be caused by minor allergies or dust, thick colored discharge usually indicates a bacterial or viral infection. Common causes include feline conjunctivitis, corneal ulcers, or upper respiratory infections (cat flu).

Is it an Emergency?

Green eye discharge is classified as a Medium Urgency level. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, eye conditions in cats can progress very quickly. If left untreated, a simple infection can lead to painful corneal ulcers or even permanent vision loss. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If your cat is squinting, pawing at the eye, or if the eye looks cloudy, seek veterinary care more urgently.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's eye can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. Cats often groom themselves frequently, potentially wiping away the discharge before they arrive at the clinic. A photo allows the vet to see the original color, thickness, and volume of the discharge, as well as the degree of swelling or redness present when the symptoms were at their peak. This helps them determine exactly how quickly your pet needs to be seen.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications on a cat without veterinary approval. Many human eye drops contain ingredients that can be irritating or even toxic to feline eyes.

faqs.question: How should I clean the discharge in the meantime?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the discharge using a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Use a fresh cloth for each eye to avoid spreading the infection.

faqs.question: Is green eye discharge contagious to other cats?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Many causes of green discharge, such as feline herpesvirus or calicivirus, are highly contagious between cats. It is best to isolate the affected cat until you see a vet.
